topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. It was a formal offer letter, crisp and professional, detailing a position at a company I’d only seen advertised months ago. My stomach twisted into a tight, cold knot. I reread the bullet points, the impressive salary figures, the title that sounded so much more substantial than the freelance gig my friend, Sarah, had been describing. A slow, simmering anger began to bubble up, hot and corrosive. I stared at the screen, my jaw clenching so hard my molars ached. When Sarah finally called, her voice was bright, almost sickeningly cheerful. "Guess what? I got the job! It’s amazing!" The sheer audacity of the deception made my hands tremble. I couldn't meet her eyes; I just gripped my coffee mug until the ceramic threatened to shatter.

[story 2]

I watched him across the crowded bar, the easy confidence in his posture grating on my nerves like sandpaper. He spoke of quarterly reports and executive meetings with such practiced ease, painting a picture of a high-powered career that felt impossibly distant from the reality I’d pieced together. Every casual mention of his 'new responsibilities' was a fresh sting. A sharp tension built behind my ribs, a pressure that made me want to physically recoil. When he laughed at a story about a project, it sounded hollow, a brittle performance. I took a slow sip of my drink, the ice clinking loudly, and felt a distinct hardening in my chest. I wanted to say something cutting, something that would expose the falsehood, but the words caught in my throat, replaced by a stiff, unwavering silence.

[story 3]

The proof was small: a hastily deleted draft document on his shared drive, a file name that screamed 'Project Phoenix,' clearly belonging to the company he claimed to be consulting for. Seeing it confirmed everything. A wave of intense irritation washed over me, leaving a metallic taste in my mouth. I didn't confront him immediately. Instead, I packed my bag with deliberate, slow movements, each action precise and cold. As I walked out of the coffee shop, the noise of the street seemed muted, and I felt a strange, focused clarity. My shoulders were rigid, and I walked with a deliberate stride, every step a testament to the deep displeasure churning inside me. I knew exactly what I needed to do, and the resolve to execute it felt heavy and absolute.
